target.name: Featurely
target.tagline: Replace 5 tools with one dashboard for indie developers.

uxSummary: Featurely is positioned cleanly as a Vercel/Neon-hosted indie-dev admin suite — one dashboard replacing feedback, errors, uptime, flags, analytics, i18n, and a CMS-ish 'Versions / Translations / Maintenance mode' grab-bag. The visual language is restrained: a dark theme with one accent yellow used both for the F logomark and the primary CTA, with thin yellow underlines beneath badges that match the brand mark — that consistency is unusually disciplined for an indie. Pricing is unusually candid for a Hobby tier ('genuinely free with no trial expiry') and the side-by-side compare-plans table is the strongest pricing page detail. The blog has poster-style typographic covers ('STOP JUGGLING SAAS TOOLS', 'AI GENERATED SLOP'), which read as a deliberate voice — a value system, not just feature posts. The /news page is clearly the developer-facing changelog.

highlights.label: Pricing transparency
highlights.note: The FAQ line 'No surprise charges — billing only activates when you turn it on' is the kind of detail that lands with indie buyers. Combined with the explicit '€15/seat/mo' line in the compare-plans table, it sets up trust before the user even tries the product.

highlights.label: Brand consistency across surfaces
highlights.note: The F mark, accent yellow, and the thin yellow underline-stripe show up identically on the home hero, the blog covers, the pricing CTA, and the /demo Enter button. Most indies break visual consistency at the blog; Featurely holds it.

highlights.label: Blog-as-positioning
highlights.note: Post titles ('Stop Juggling SaaS Tools', 'AI Generated Slop', 'Proactive Error Tracking', 'Public Roadmaps') are a manifesto stack. They wedge the product into a worldview rather than walking through features.

highlights.label: Compare-plans honesty
highlights.note: Hobby gets 1 project / 1 team member / 15-min uptime check interval; Indie gets 1 project but 5-min checks at €12/mo. That granular asymmetry signals a founder who actually thinks about what an indie at each stage will use.

frictionPoints.label: /demo 'Enter the live demo' never lands
frictionPoints.note: Clicking the primary CTA on /demo flips the button to a disabled 'Signing you in…' state with a spinner. We waited 6+ seconds and the button remained stuck — no redirect, no error, no console message. See Scenario 3 screenshot. This is the loudest UX failure on the site: a paid visitor would assume the product is broken and bounce. The free-account fallback is at /sign-up but is a 7-route detour away from the headline value prop.

frictionPoints.label: /sign-up Create account submit fires no network request
frictionPoints.note: Filled name/email/password on /sign-up, clicked 'Create account', the button flipped to disabled 'Please wait…' — and stayed there for 10+ seconds. Network panel showed zero fetch/XHR calls. Either the client handler is awaiting a promise that never resolves, or a server action is being invoked through a transport that's silently dropping. Either way, the registration funnel is currently 100% blocked unless the user picks 'Continue with Google'. We did not test the Google OAuth path.

frictionPoints.label: Cookie banner covers the page on first paint
frictionPoints.note: The 'Cookie preferences' modal is a centered overlay (not a footer banner) and blocks the hero on first load. Dismissed via 'Accept all cookies' before each public screenshot, but a visitor who's banner-blind will scroll past it without realising the hero CTA is behind it.

testingStruggles.label: Detecting that the /demo button auto-disables in a re-entered session
testingStruggles.note: On a returning browser context, /demo's Enter button lands already-disabled (a previously-pending demo session). On a fresh runner context, the button starts enabled and only disables on click. The test had to be reshaped to fire-and-forget the click without awaiting, then wait 800ms for React to flip the disabled state, then screenshot — otherwise either (a) the click missed because the button was already disabled, or (b) a hard navigation kicked off and the screenshot caught a blank in-between frame.

testingStruggles.label: Empty Page caught on post-click navigation
testingStruggles.note: An earlier version of the test waited for waitForURL after clicking Enter — Playwright's URL resolver fired before the destination page had rendered, producing a 1280×720 fully-black PNG. Switched to a fixed 800ms wait after the click and screenshot before any navigation could complete.

testingStruggles.label: Console-error assertion flagged build as 'failed' despite all screenshots being clean
testingStruggles.note: The runner's default 'fail on console errors' assertion tripped on /api/projects/.../analytics/events/batch returning 401. That endpoint is Featurely's own analytics SDK, which is correctly designed to 401 for anonymous visitors — it's a known-good 401, not a real failure. Disabling the console-error assertion for demo-style runs would clean this up.

skippedRoutes.reason: /sign-up form submit is broken (see frictionPoints) and the /demo Enter button is stuck (see frictionPoints). No path into authenticated surface from anonymous tooling.
Would only land us back at sign-up since registration never completes.
OAuth flow redirects to accounts.google.com which Playwright cannot complete from a clean runner identity.
On a separate subdomain — not in the demo baseline scope.
